sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 80, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Starting level 1. The board shows several colored objects on a gray background with a pink bar at the bottom (likely a HUD/timer). Need to identify objects and their relationships.
- Plan: First, inspect the segmentation to understand what objects exist, then probe actions to understand the game mechanics.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 79 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The board didn't change. The player couldn't move LEFT. The left frame at col 16-19 is blocking the player's LEFT movement.
Let me think about this differently. The player is at col 20-23, and the left frame is at col 16-19. The player can't move LEFT because the frame is blocking.
So the player can't move LEFT (blocked by left frame), can't move UP (blocked by blue square token). The player can move RIGHT or DOWN.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to collapse the W part (press SPACE) to make the player smaller, then move LEFT/UP.
Actually, let me reconsider the frame collection mechanic. Looking at the right frame collection:
- Player was at row 32-35, col 44-47
- Right frame was at row 24-27, col 44-47
- Player moved UP to row 28-31, col 44-47
- The player's new position (row 28) was just below the frame's bottom (row 27)
- The frame was collected
For the left frame:
- Left frame is at row 28-31, col 16-19
- To collect it, the player needs to move to row 32 (just below the frame's bottom at row 31)
- But the player is currently at row 28-35, col 20-23
- The player's W part is at row 28-32, which overlaps with the left frame's row range (28-31)
Wait, the player's W part is at row 28-32, and the left frame is at row 28-31. They overlap in rows 28-31. But the player is at col 20-23, and the left frame is at col 16-19. They don't overlap in columns.
Maybe the frame is collected when the player's W part overlaps with the frame's position. The player's W part is at row 28-32, col 20-23. The left frame is at row 28-31, col 16-19. They don't overlap.
Let me try moving DOWN to get the player below the left frame, then move LEFT to align with the left frame, then move UP to collect it.
Actually, the player is at row 28-35, col 20-23. The left frame is at row 28-31, col 16-19. If the player moves DOWN, the player would be at row 32-39, col 20-23. Then the player can move LEFT to col 16-19. Then the player is at row 32-39, col 16-19, which is below the left frame at row 28-31. Then the player can move UP to collect the left frame.
But the blue square token is attached to the player and would block the UP movement.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to collapse the W part (SPACE) to remove the blue square token, then move UP.
Actually, looking at the blue square token more carefully: when the player's W part is expanded (16 pixels), the blue square token is inside the W part. When the player's W part is collapsed (4 pixels), the blue square token is outside the W part.
When the right frame was collected, the player's W part was collapsed (4 pixels). The player didn't have a blue square token.
When the bottom frame was collected, the player's W part was collapsed (4 pixels). The player didn't have a blue square token.
So the frames are collected when the player's W part is collapsed (4 pixels) and the player doesn't have a blue square token.
The blue square token appeared after the first SPACE action. Before that, the player didn't have a blue square token.
So maybe the solution is to somehow remove the blue square token. But I don't know how to remove it.
Let me try pressing SPACE to collapse the W part, then see if the blue square token disappears.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try collapsing W part to see if blue square token disappears
# First check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("Current player W pixels:", [n for n in seg if n['color'] == 'W'][0]['pixels'])

# Press SPACE to collapse W
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
